g.data-narration: Welcome to Equivalence and Minimization of Automata - a crucial topic in Theory of Computation.
When we design automata to recognize languages, there are often redundant states that don't affect the language recognized.
g.text: When designing automata, redundant states may exist that don't affect language recognition.
Minimization removes these redundant states, producing an efficient, optimal DFA.
Applications: Compiler design, Pattern matching, Digital circuits

g.data-narration: We will explore three key concepts: equivalent states, distinguishable states, and the Myhill-Nerode theorem.
Two states p and q are equivalent if for every input string w, both reach states that are either both accepting or both non-accepting.
g.text: Two states p and q are equivalent if:
For all strings w ∈ Σ*: δ(p,w) and δ(q,w) are both accepting OR both non-accepting

g.data-narration: States are distinguishable if there exists a string w where one reaches a final state and the other reaches a non-final state.
g.text: Two states p and q are distinguishable if:
There exists a string w such that one reaches a final state and the other reaches a non-final state

g.data-narration: The Myhill-Nerode theorem states that the minimum number of states equals the number of equivalence classes in the relation R_L.
g.text: The minimum number of states in a DFA for language L equals:
Number of equivalence classes in R_L relation

g.data-narration: The minimization algorithm uses partition refinement: start with accepting/non-accepting, then iteratively split until stable.
Start by separating accepting states from non-accepting states - this is the foundation of minimization.
In refinement, check if states in the same block have transitions to different blocks on any input symbol - if so, split them.
g.text: For each block, check if states have transitions to different blocks:
If δ(p,a) and δ(q,a) go to different blocks for any symbol a, split p and q into separate blocks

g.data-narration: Repeat the refinement step until no more splits occur - the partition becomes stable and minimization is complete.
Let's work through a complete example. Given a DFA with states q0-q3, final state q3, on alphabet {0,1}.
On input '0', q0 goes to q1 and q2 goes to q3 - they go to different blocks, so q0 and q2 are distinguishable.
Checking q0 and q1 on input '1' - q0 goes to q2 and q1 goes to q3 - different blocks! They must be split.
To check if two DFAs are equivalent, we can use the product automaton or minimize both and compare their structures.
g.text: Find state (p,q) where p∈F₁, q∉F₂ or vice versa
Minimize both DFAs, compare structure (up to isomorphism)
Example: DFA₁ (odd 'a's) vs DFA₂ (even 'a's) — String "a" distinguishes: DFA₁ accepts, DFA₂ rejects → NOT equivalent

g.data-narration: For NFAs, convert to DFAs first using subset construction, then minimize both DFAs to check equivalence.
In summary: equivalent states behave identically, distinguishable states differ, and minimization produces a unique optimal DFA.
